content:
Ryan, I get why this weighs on you. Nobody wants to saddle their kids—or their faith—with needless social baggage.

A few thoughts, holding firmly to Scripture’s authority:

- Inerrancy isn’t the same thing as wooden literalism. We submit to what the text actually affirms, in the way it intends to affirm it. Scripture often speaks in ordinary, phenomenological language (sunrise/sunset, “the ends of the earth”) without intending a technical, modern-science claim. That isn’t error; it’s how truthful human language works.

- About the “firmament” (Hebrew raqia): faithful scholars who love and trust the Bible do not all agree it teaches a solid dome. Many understand raqia as “expanse/sky,” the space God made by separating waters below (seas) from waters above (clouds/heavenly waters)—a description from an earthbound vantage point. Others see ancient-cosmic imagery used to teach real theological truths: God orders, names, rules, and fills. In any case, Genesis 1’s clear affirmations are that God created everything by His word, set boundaries, and made the heavens to declare His glory—not that it’s giving a modern physics map of the cosmos.

- God is indeed “big enough to be honored either way” when our aim is faithfulness. He is honored when Christians, with humble consciences shaped by Scripture, land in different places on secondary matters after real study (Romans 14). What God is not honored by is trimming our convictions merely to avoid embarrassment (Galatians 1:10), or insisting on a reading the text itself does not require.

- Don’t add offense the gospel doesn’t require. The cross will always be a stumbling block; we don’t need to make Christianity harder by tying the truth of Scripture to a specific cosmological model that Scripture may not be asserting. Keep first things first: creation by God, the goodness of creation, the image of God in humanity, the reality of sin, and the Lordship of Christ.

- Teach your kids courage and charity. It’s okay to say: “Christians agree God made the heavens and the earth. Some think Genesis is using everyday sky-language; others think it’s stricter. We won’t be ashamed of Scripture, but we also won’t be combative. We follow the evidence in the text and speak respectfully.”

If, after careful study, you become convinced the Bible truly teaches a specific view of the firmament, then bear any social cost with quiet integrity (Luke 9:26). But don’t take on cost for something Scripture doesn’t actually demand. Do the work of exegesis (2 Timothy 2:15), hold your conclusions with appropriate weight, and keep the offense where God put it—on the gospel, not on avoidable shibboleths.

If it helps, I can point you to solid, inerrancy-affirming resources that handle raqia carefully from different angles so you can weigh them with an open Bible.
